rationale: The citing paper uses Bottomley and Holden (2001) as part of its core theoretical baseline that brand extension evaluations are primarily fit-mediated. This is not a stray bibliography mention: the citation appears in the main motivation and again in the general discussion to define the prior view the authors are repositioning. The paper's contribution is framed as showing boundary conditions and an alternative affect-based route that can influence evaluations even when core-extension similarity is low, unless similarity is made salient first.
relation_description: The citing paper treats the cited paper as evidence for the established fit-based account of brand extension evaluation, then qualifies that account by introducing an affect-driven process that can operate independently of perceived fit. In this sense, it narrows when the cited paper's fit-centric implication holds.
